text:
Getting Started with Rope
Basics for Any Play
Before getting into how to actually tie or be tied, spend time learning how to establish boundaries, identify safe play partners and environments, and negotiate for the play you want.
Navigating the Rope Community
Crash Restraint provides a one-page handout about common pitfalls while navigating the rope community.
Maintain a Healthy Skepticism
There are no standards for rope education, and a tremendous amount of misinformation and confusion gets passed around. Attending classes at reputable community venues does not necessarily mean the information is correct. Instructors can be hired to teach at top-tier venues or websites even if their content is misleading or omits crucial safety information. Absorb what you can with a grain of salt. Ask whether what you are told makes sense and whether it agrees with what you have learned from other sources. In person, ask lots of "why" questions; if an instructor cannot answer, they may be repeating material without understanding it.
You Are Not the Problem
The first style of rope you encounter might not work for your body or relationship dynamic. The possibilities are broad: any body can be tied, and rope can be part of a scene with any mood. Do not let other people dictate how you should experience rope. Persist in finding or inventing what works for you.
Additional Resources for Bottoms
Crash Restraint encourages bottoms to learn some tying and review safety information, while noting that the author does not regularly bottom and therefore recommends learning bottoming skills from experienced bottoming educators.
Screening Partners
Crash Restraint highly recommends using the Rope Bottoms' Share Group to check people before letting them tie you. Predators may move between cities after bans or establish themselves as community leaders. Checking potential partners through the group and by asking other local bottoms is not a silver bullet, but can greatly reduce risk.
title: Getting Started with Rope
